original_hypothesis.title: setOwner Can Leave Hidden Extra OWNER Role Holders Due to EnumerableSet Assumptions
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: setOwner() performs grantRole(OWNER, newOwner) then revokeRole(OWNER, getRoleMember(OWNER, 0)). If an OWNER previously called grantRole(OWNER, anotherAddress) directly (which is possible since OWNER is DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE's own admin), the EnumerableSet contains multiple members. setOwner only revokes index 0, potentially leaving additional OWNER-role holders hidden from the owner() view function, which only returns getRoleMember(OWNER, 0).
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The single-owner invariant is critical for upgrade security (UUPS) and overall governance. Hidden OWNER addresses could authorize malicious upgrades or role changes invisible to the protocol's nominal owner.

proof_of_signal.pattern: grantRole(OWNER, newOwner) adds to set, revokeRole(OWNER, getRoleMember(OWNER, 0)) removes index 0 only; no check that getRoleMemberCount(OWNER) == 1 before or after

preconditions: An OWNER has previously called grantRole(DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE, anotherAddress) directly
getRoleMemberCount(OWNER) > 1 at time of setOwner call

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: setOwner adds one member and removes one (index 0), net zero change in count if count > 1
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: OWNER grants role to hidden address, setOwner transfers ownership but hidden address retains OWNER role
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Hidden owner can authorize UUPS upgrade to malicious implementation, draining all token balances

economic_questions: Can getRoleMemberCount(OWNER) ever exceed 1 in normal operations?
Does the deployment script or initialization create multiple OWNER members?
What is the impact of a hidden OWNER performing unauthorized upgrades?
validation_requirements: Test: grant OWNER to address B, then call setOwner(C), verify B retains OWNER role
Verify owner() only shows index 0, hiding other OWNER holders

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Full protocol TVL via malicious upgrade
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Two transactions: grant hidden owner + wait for setOwner

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete protocol compromise if hidden owner performs upgrade

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Potential total loss of holdings via malicious upgrade

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check deployment script: deployToken.js calls setOwner(INITIAL_OWNER_ADDRESS) after initialize()
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify EnumerableSet behavior on add/remove with multiple members

validation_result.mechanism: EnumerableSet single-member assumption violation in setOwner
validation_result.impact_summary: An OWNER can plant hidden additional OWNER role holders via direct grantRole calls. setOwner only removes index 0, leaving hidden holders with full OWNER privileges including UUPS upgrade authority, invisible to the owner() view function.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The setOwner function at Token.sol:77-82 performs grantRole(OWNER, newOwner) followed by revokeRole(OWNER, getRoleMember(OWNER, 0)). It implicitly assumes getRoleMemberCount(OWNER) == 1, but there is no enforcement of this invariant anywhere in the codebase. Since OWNER is aliased to DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE and DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE is its own role admin (by OZ default), any current OWNER can call the inherited public grantRole(DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE, someAddress) to add additional OWNER members. When setOwner is subsequently called, it adds one member and removes one member (at index 0), leaving all other members intact. The owner() view function only returns getRoleMember(OWNER, 0), hiding any additional holders. Those hidden holders retain full OWNER privileges including _authorizeUpgrade for UUPS upgrades.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Token.sol lines 77-82 show the defective setOwner function. Token.sol line 84 shows owner() only returns index 0. OpenZeppelin's AccessControlUpgradeable.grantRole is public with onlyRole(getRoleAdmin(role)), and since getRoleAdmin(DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE) == DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE, any current OWNER can call it to add more OWNER members. No override prevents direct grantRole(OWNER, ...) calls. The code lacks any check that getRoleMemberCount(OWNER) == 1 either as a precondition or postcondition in setOwner.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: After grantRole adds newOwner, if the set already has >1 members, revokeRole only removes the member at index 0 (which may not be the caller due to OZ EnumerableSet swap-and-pop ordering). Remaining members retain OWNER role. No check ensures getRoleMemberCount(OWNER) == 1 before or after.
Only returns the member at index 0 of the OWNER role set. If multiple members exist, the additional members are completely hidden from this view function, creating a false sense of single ownership.

calling_sequence: 1. Current OWNER (A) calls grantRole(OWNER, B) - succeeds because OWNER is admin of itself
4. grantRole(OWNER, C) adds C to set: [A, B, C]
6. OZ EnumerableSet swap-and-pop: C moves to index 0, set becomes [C, B]
7. owner() returns C (index 0), but B retains full OWNER role
8. B can call _authorizeUpgrade, grantRole, revokeRole, setOwner, etc.
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The attack starts with the inherited public grantRole function from AccessControlEnumerableUpgradeable. Since OWNER = DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE and getRoleAdmin(DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE) = DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE (OZ default), any OWNER holder passes the onlyRole(getRoleAdmin(OWNER)) check. After adding an extra member, setOwner at Token.sol:77-82 only removes one member (index 0), leaving the extra member with full privileges. The _authorizeUpgrade function at Token.sol:88 only checks onlyRole(OWNER), which the hidden member passes.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Deployment creates single OWNER via initialize + setOwner
config_validation.deployment_evidence: deployToken.js calls initialize() (grants OWNER to deployer msg.sender, count=1), then calls setOwner(INITIAL_OWNER_ADDRESS) which transfers to the intended owner (count=1 after). Deployment itself is clean, but the vulnerability exists in post-deployment operation when the OWNER can call grantRole directly.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Scenario is fully valid: (1) Current OWNER A calls grantRole(DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE, B) - this is a public inherited function that A is authorized to call since OWNER=DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE is its own admin. (2) Set becomes [A, B]. (3) A calls setOwner(C). grantRole adds C: set = [A, B, C]. revokeRole(getRoleMember(OWNER, 0)) removes A: OZ swap-and-pop moves C to index 0, set = [C, B]. (4) owner() returns C, but B retains hasRole(OWNER) == true. (5) B can call upgradeToAndCall via _authorizeUpgrade(onlyRole(OWNER)) to deploy a malicious implementation. This is a realistic insider threat or key compromise scenario where a departing/malicious owner plants a backdoor before ownership transfer.
validation_result.impact_assessment: Critical. A hidden OWNER holder retains the ability to: (1) authorize UUPS upgrades to arbitrary malicious implementations, potentially draining all token balances, (2) grant/revoke any role including ADMIN, MASTER_MINTER, CONTROLLER, (3) call setOwner to take visible ownership, (4) perform all DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE privileged operations. The owner() view function provides false assurance of single ownership. For a regulated stablecoin, this constitutes a complete protocol compromise vector.
recommended_mitigations: Add a check in setOwner: require(getRoleMemberCount(OWNER) == 1, 'unexpected multiple owners') before performing the transfer, or iterate to remove all existing OWNER members except the new one
Override grantRole and revokeRole for the OWNER role to enforce the single-owner invariant, preventing direct grantRole(OWNER, ...) calls and forcing all ownership changes through setOwner
Add a require(getRoleMemberCount(OWNER) == 1) postcondition assertion at the end of setOwner
Consider implementing OpenZeppelin's Ownable2StepUpgradeable pattern instead of manual role-based ownership for the upgrade authority
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy EURFToken via proxy, call initialize(). 2. Call setOwner(ownerA). Verify getRoleMemberCount(DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE)==1. 3. As ownerA, call grantRole(DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE, hiddenB). Verify getRoleMemberCount==2. 4. As ownerA, call setOwner(ownerC). 5. Assert owner()==ownerC. 6. Assert hasRole(DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE, hiddenB)==true (hidden owner persists). 7. Assert getRoleMemberCount(DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE)==2. 8. As hiddenB, call upgradeToAndCall with a malicious implementation to prove full control.
uncertainties: The exact swap-and-pop behavior of OZ EnumerableSet determines which member ends up at index 0 after removal, but the core issue (extra members remain) is independent of ordering
additional_testing_needed: Verify that no other code path in the system calls _revokeRole or renounceRole for OWNER that might clean up extra members
Test the specific EnumerableSet ordering after add/remove sequences to confirm which address ends up at index 0
Verify that renounceRole(OWNER, caller) is not blocked, as it could be used by the legitimate owner to remove themselves, leaving only the hidden owner
